title: How to Block Out Snoring With Sound Masking
meta_title: How to Block Out Snoring With Sound Masking (2026 Guide)
description: Snoring peaks in the low frequencies, so ordinary white noise misses it. Use brown noise or rain layered over brown noise, set just above the snoring volume, with an all-night timer. Setup steps in DRMN.
lede: You tried white noise and still heard every snore. That is a frequency problem, not a volume problem — and it has a frequency solution.
cta_tagline: Brown noise, rain, and a 10-hour timer — free on the App Store
answer_html: Short answer: snoring lives in the low frequencies, mostly below 500 Hz. Mask it with sound that also lives there: brown noise alone, or rain layered over brown noise. Set the volume just above the snoring's average level at your pillow and run it all night with DRMN's 10-hour timer.
body_html:
Why white noise fails against snoring

White noise spreads its energy evenly from low frequencies to high. Snoring doesn't: it rumbles, concentrated in the bottom of the spectrum. When you play white noise against a snore, only a small slice of the noise overlaps the snore's range, so the snore punches through. Turning up the volume eventually covers it — at a level that hisses you awake instead.

Brown noise flips the equation. Its energy piles up exactly where snoring sits, so it blurs the snore into the background at a volume you can comfortably sleep in.

The masking hierarchy for snoring

OptionHow well it covers snoringNotes
Brown noiseBestLow-frequency energy matches the snore's range
Rain + brown noise mixBest, more naturalRain adds texture; brown noise carries the low end
Fan noiseGoodDecent low-mid coverage, familiar to fan sleepers
Pink noiseFairSofter than white, still light in the lowest range
White noiseWeak at safe volumeNeeds to be loud to cover lows

Set up the anti-snoring mix in DRMN

  1. Download Sound Machine Deep Sleep DRMN free from the App Store.
  2. Start Brown Noise from the library.
  3. Open the mixer and layer Rain on top at a lower volume, if pure brown noise feels too flat.
  4. With your partner snoring, raise the volume one step at a time until the snore blurs into the noise. Stop there — you want to mask the average, not the loudest peak.
  5. Set the sleep timer to 8–10 hours so masking lasts as long as the snoring does, and press play.

Position helps too: put the phone or a Bluetooth speaker on your side of the bed, between your ear and the snorer. Sound masking works best when the masker is closer to you than the noise.
faq_html:
What sound blocks out snoring best?

Brown noise, because its energy sits in the same low range as the snore. Rain over brown noise covers the same range and sounds more natural.

How loud should the masking be?

Just above the snoring's average level at your pillow. Masking blurs the snore into the background; it does not need to drown the peaks.

Does white noise cover snoring?

Only at uncomfortable volume. Its energy spreads across all frequencies, so little lands where the snore lives. Brown noise does it quieter.

faq.question: What sound blocks out snoring best?
faq.answer: Brown noise. Snoring concentrates its energy below roughly 500 Hz, and brown noise puts its energy in the same low range, so it covers snoring at a lower overall volume than white noise. Rain layered over brown noise works as well and sounds more natural.

faq.question: How loud should masking sound be to cover snoring?
faq.answer: Just above the snoring's average level at your pillow, not its loudest peak. Start moderate and raise it one step at a time. Masking works by blurring the snore into the background, not by out-shouting it.

faq.question: Does white noise cover snoring?
faq.answer: Partially. White noise spreads energy evenly across all frequencies, so only a fraction lands in the low range where snoring lives. To cover snoring with white noise you must play it uncomfortably loud; brown noise does the job quieter.

card_title: How to Block Out Snoring With Sound Masking
card_description: Snoring is low-frequency noise, so most white noise fails. Here is the mix that actually covers it.
